svg.text: FFT-grid sensitivity is not one deep-sidelobe story

text.tspan: This follow-up reruns the FFT-density audit on two deep-sidelobe windows instead of only the Kaiser family. Same length 129, same dense

svg.text: Peak sidelobe error versus FFT size

text.tspan: Kaiser drifts hard, Blackman-Harris stays nearly locked, and

svg.text: Main-lobe width error versus FFT size

text.tspan: Nuttall is the interesting surprise here: the width read can
stay wrong after the sidelobe read is already mostly calm.
Blackman-Harris keeps landing almost exactly on the same
sampled peak. Nuttall and Kaiser do not.
This is why the Nuttall width read stays soft longer: at 512
and 1024 the first local minimum can jump to the wrong
At 512 points, Kaiser β=8.6 is still the harsh anchor: 4.93 dB of sidelobe error and 1.18 bins of width error. Blackman-Harris is effectively grid-stable at the same probe (0.01 dB,
0.02 bins) because both its first null and first sidelobe peak land almost exactly on sampled locations. Nuttall changes the family read instead of replaying Kaiser: its sidelobe
error at 512 is only 0.35 dB, but its first-null miss is 0.21 bins, so the width read stays much softer than the sidelobe read.
